title: Chapter 14: The Rooftop
content: On Day Eleven, I heard the gunshot.

Not the *crack* of Derek's 9mm—by now I knew that sound the way you know your alarm clock. This was different. Deeper. A rifle, with the compressed boom of a suppressed round and the distant *ping* of a bullet hitting metal.
It came from above. From the roof.

Luna heard it too—her head snapped toward the ceiling like a hunting dog on point, her nostrils flaring, her body going taut with alertness. But she didn't move toward the sound. She moved toward *me*, placing herself between me and the door with the instinctive protectiveness that had become second nature.
"Gun," she said. "Big gun. Not Derek's."
"No kidding."

Another shot. This one was followed by something that made my blood run cold—a zombie on the floor below us, one of the shamblers that had been lurking in the stairwell, dropped with a wet thud. Headshot. Through the ceiling.
Someone on the roof was *sniping zombies*.
I looked at Luna. She looked at me.
"Go look?" she asked.
"Go look."

The roof access door was on the east side of Floor 15, past a hallway I'd never explored because the top floor had been the first to lose power and the last to get any residual light. Luna led the way, her night-vision superior to mine, one hand reached back to guide me through the dark.
The door to the roof was locked from the outside—a heavy chain threaded through the push bar, secured with a padlock that had been shot through. The chain hung loose, clinking softly in the wind.
"Someone's living up here," I whispered.
Luna nodded. She could hear what I couldn't—movement above us, careful footsteps, the click of a rifle bolt.
We climbed through.

The roof of Crimson Ridge was flat, square, and open to the sky—an acre of concrete surrounded by a waist-high parapet. Water towers on the north side. Ventilation units on the south. And in the center, pitched between two ventilation housings like a camping tent in the world's worst national park, a bivouac made of emergency blankets and scavenged furniture.
Standing at the parapet, rifle shouldered, was a woman I'd never seen before.

She was tall—taller than me, which wasn't saying much, but she carried it like a weapon. Lean and angular, with sharp features and dark hair cut short enough to be practical. She wore cargo pants, a thermal undershirt, and a surplus military jacket that hung off her frame. Her eyes were behind a pair of binoculars, and she was tracking movement on the street below with the focused patience of someone who'd been watching for a long time.
She hadn't noticed us yet. Or she had and didn't care.
I cleared my throat.

She turned. Smooth, unhurried, the rifle swinging in a controlled arc that brought the muzzle toward my center mass without quite pointing at it. Her eyes were dark and flat, assessing us the way a hawk assesses field mice—professionally, without malice.
"Sorry," I said, hands up. "Door was open. I live on Floor 12."
"Alex Chen, Apartment 12B," she said. Not a question.
My turn to be surprised. "How do you know—"

"I've been watching you for three days. You leave your light on at night—stupid, by the way, it attracts them. You've got a reinforced door that someone installed while you were sleeping, which is either très suspicious or très interesting. And you have a zombie girl who carries a fire axe like it's made of paper." She looked at Luna. "Hi."
Luna stared back, unblinking. "Hello."
"You can talk. That's new." The woman's eyebrows rose a fraction of an inch—her version of slack-jawed shock. "When did you start talking?"
"Four days ago," Luna said. "Alex helps."
"Alex helps," the woman repeated, and there was something in her voice—not mockery, but a complicated emotion I couldn't identify. Wonder, maybe. Or envy.
"Who are you?" I asked.

"Mia Santos." She lowered the rifle, slinging it over her shoulder with the practiced ease of someone who'd been carrying firearms since childhood. " competitive shooter since I was twelve. Army brat. Prepper—I was *that* kid in high school with the bug-out bag, and everyone laughed, and now everyone's dead, and I'm not."
She said the last part without satisfaction. Just fact, cold and hard as the concrete beneath our feet.
"How long have you been up here?" I asked.

"Since Day Two. I was visiting my brother—he lived on 15C. He didn't make it." A shadow crossed her face, there and gone. "I've been watching the building. Mapping movements. Counting the dead. You'd be amazed how much you can see from up here when you're not busy dying."
"What did you see?"
She looked at me. Really looked, for the first time—the full diagnostic sweep, top to bottom, cataloguing my condition the way she'd catalogued the building.

"I saw you save a girl from the guy on Floor 8. I saw your zombie girlfriend throw a couch like it was a pillow. I saw you bring two women into your apartment and not harm either of them." The rifle stayed on her shoulder. Her hand stayed near the trigger. "In an apocalypse full of monsters, you're the first human I've seen who acts like one."
"That's the nicest thing anyone's ever said to me."
"Don't get sentimental. I'm not joining your harem."
"I don't have a—"

"The system on your wrist." She pointed at my watch. "I've been watching it glow through your windows at night. It's some kind of... structured reward system, right? You do things, you get things. My dad was in psyops. I know a behavioral conditioning rig when I see one."

My mouth opened and closed. She'd figured out the system from *three days of surveillance through a window*. Mia Santos was either a genius or a paranoid, and in the apocalypse, those were the same thing.
"It's called the Romance System," I said, because lying to someone who'd been spying on me for three days seemed counterproductive. "It gives me rewards for... connecting with people. Emotionally."
"Romance," she repeated flatly. "You get rewarded for *romance*. In the zombie apocalypse."
"I know how it sounds."

"It sounds like a trap." She leaned against the parapet, arms crossed. "Something that gives you things for falling in love? That's not a blessing. That's a leash. The question is: who's holding the other end?"
I didn't have an answer. I'd been avoiding that question since Day One, because I wasn't sure I'd like the answer.
"Fair point," I said. "But right now, the leash is feeding me, protecting my people, and keeping me alive. I'll worry about who's holding it when I'm not dodging zombies and gun-toting gym bros."
Mia almost smiled. The corner of her mouth twitched—the same ghost-expression I'd seen on Sarah's face. "Practical. I like practical."
"Does that mean you'll help us?"
"Help you do what, exactly?"
"Defeat Derek. Secure the building. Keep the people I've saved safe. Long-term, figure out what the hell happened to the world and whether anyone's coming to fix it."

She considered this for a long moment, the wind whipping her short hair, the rifle gleaming on her shoulder. Below us, the city spread out in ruins—fires in the distance, the occasional gunshot from other buildings, the shambling multitudes of the dead filling streets that had once been alive with traffic.

"I've been alone for nine days," she said. "I've got a rifle with eleven rounds, a canteen of rainwater, and three packages of MREs. I can see everything in this building, but I can't change anything from up here. I've been waiting for a reason to come down."
"And?"
"And you're the first reason I've found."
She extended her hand. Her grip was firm, calloused, professional—the handshake of someone who understood that words were cheap and actions were currency.
"I'm not romancing you," she said. "I'm not a quest target. I'm a *partner*. Equal terms. Clear?"
"Crystal."

**ROMANCE SYSTEM — SIGNAL DETECTED**
**TARGET: MIA SANTOS**
**DIFFICULTY: ★★★☆☆**
**STATUS: Potential Romance Target (Not yet available for quest)**
**NOTE: Target has established boundary conditions. Romance quest will not trigger until trust threshold is met organically. Forced progression will trigger Hollow Heart penalty.**
I read the notification and mentally filed it. Mia Santos wasn't a game to be won—she'd made that clear, and the system agreed. She was an ally. A partner. And God knew we needed one.
"Welcome to the team," I said.
She snorted. "Some team. A gamer, a zombie, a medical student, and a traumatized teenager. Against a building full of dead people and a psychopath with a gun."
"Don't forget the cat."
"God. Forget the cat. If we're counting the cat, we're already doomed."
We went downstairs together. And for the first time since the outbreak, the weight on my shoulders felt a little less crushing.
We weren't just surviving anymore.
We were building something.
